input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A 1/10-scale teleoperated autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technology, featuring lower and upper chassis layers and autonomy elements as main hardware components. Operates within the Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) domain of Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS), characterized by resource-constrained electronic control units (ECUs) requiring offloading of critical computations and safety-critical operations.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides Data Monitoring (real-time visualization of vehicle state data, projected driving path, detected object tracks; UI for viewing, saving, and generating reports), Computation Offloading (offloading ACC system to cloud for lead vehicle detection, speed calculation based on distance, and actuation commands), and Remote Management (enabling/disabling ACC, setting desired speed, emergency braking commands). Target users include operators for monitoring and management, and developers for troubleshooting via data collection.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 SDK for GPU-accelerated processing, Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) as middleware for component communication, Python for data processing scripts, C++ for real-time control modules, and MATLAB/Simulink for simulation and model-based design of the ACC system.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system requires 100 ms clock synchronization accuracy for device coordination to enable computation offloading, as dictated by hardware limitations; while safety-critical vehicular standards typically mandate stricter synchronization (<100 ms), the system adheres to 100 ms due to current hardware constraints.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The system employs a hierarchical entity-based architecture with three distinct instances deployed on separate hardware components: User Entity (containing 3D Visualization and Control Panel sub-components), Digital Twin Entity (handling data management and control mechanisms), and Physical Twin Entity (managing sensor/actuator data pre-processing, actuation, and device communication, including Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking). This structure deviates from ISO 23247 RA by extending the Physical Twin Entity to host general automotive functionality beyond typical OME responsibilities.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The architecture follows ISO 23247 RA guidelines, implementing Software Components as ROS2 packages where each Functional Entity (FE) is encapsulated within individual ROS2 nodes. Entities (User, Digital Twin, Physical Twin) are deployed on separate hardware, with ROS2 serving as the middleware for inter-component communication. The Physical Twin Entity's expanded role, hosting both resource-specific and general automotive functions, represents a significant architectural deviation from ISO 23247 standards.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The physical actuation subsystem includes a lower-level chassis controller managing steering and speed via a Vedder Electronic Speed Controller (VESCs), with the Actuation software component converting speed commands into precise motor voltage signals. Control mechanisms operate within hardware-specific current and voltage limits, ensuring safe operation through closed-loop feedback from the digital twin.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Physical sensor data (LiDAR point clouds, motor state, steering servo position, vehicle state) is collected by the Data Collecting FE. Raw LiDAR data is processed by the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking software component. All data streams undergo serialization, message type alignment, and unit conversions via the ISO 23247 Data Translation component. Communication between software components occurs over ROS2, while physical-to-digital data transfer uses Wi-Fi with UDP transport layer.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands from the ACC Controlling FE are serialized and converted by the Data Translation component, transmitted via ROS2 network to the Actuation FE, which interfaces with physical actuators (motor, steering servo). Inter-entity communication uses Wi-Fi UDP for command delivery.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Communication between software components uses ROS2 middleware; inter-entity communication occurs over Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process for the Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) Digital Twin includes requirements definition for system specifications, architectural design following ISO 23247 Part 2's entity-based reference architecture (Observable Manufacturing Element, Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, User Entity), and implementation of services such as Data Monitoring, Computation Offloading, and Remote Management interfaces. Version control is managed through structured development phases with iterative testing against operational scenarios.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Decision support is provided through a user interface enabling remote management of ACC operations, including enabling/disabling the system, setting desired speed, and initiating emergency braking commands. Real-time data analytics process sensor inputs for decision-making, with confidence levels derived from model validation against vehicle dynamics. User interactions are facilitated via a graphical interface displaying system status and control options.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin system adheres to ISO 23247:2021 standard for manufacturing, which comprises four parts: Overview and general principles, Reference architecture, Digital representation of physical manufacturing elements, and Information exchange. Part 2 defines an entity-based reference architecture where Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs) are represented by Digital Twin Entities, with Data Collection and Device Control Entities handling data interaction, and User Entities providing interfaces for monitoring and control. Functional Entities (FEs) within each entity perform specific tasks such as data transformation, actuation, and user interfacing, with implementation choices like OPC Unified Architecture not prescribed by the standard.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: Applies ISO 26262-1:2018 functional safety standards for road vehicles, including risk assessment, safety goals, and validation processes, alongside ISO 15622:2018 performance requirements and test procedures for Adaptive Cruise Control systems to ensure safety-critical operational integrity.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

The extraction comprehensively lists the three services: Data Monitoring (visualization, reporting), Computation Offloading (lead vehicle detection, speed calculation), and Remote Management (enabling/disabling ACC, speed setting, emergency braking). It correctly identifies the target users (operators and developers). Supported by quotes: 'Data Monitoring: ... view, save, and generate reports', 'Computation Offloading: ... offloading only the ACC system', 'Remote Management: ... enabling/disabling ACC', 'operators... developers'.

The extraction correctly lists the specific software stack (Jetpack 5.0, ROS2, Python, C++, MATLAB/Simulink). While the tools are correct, the descriptions of their specific roles are slightly generalized compared to the text (e.g., text links C++ to 'lifecycle management' and MATLAB to 'point cloud clustering', whereas extraction generalizes to 'real-time control modules' and 'simulation... of the ACC system'). Supported by quotes: 'NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 as the Software Development Kit', 'Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) as the middleware', and 'Application modules are implemented in Python 3.8... C++... and MATLAB/Simulink'.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extracted characteristic is highly detailed and directly supported by the text. It accurately cites the 100 ms synchronization requirement, the reason for it (hardware limitations), and the comparison to stricter safety-critical standards found in the source requirements section. Supporting quotes: 'Components within the DTS shall have clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms' and 'We require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.'

The extraction provides a precise description of the three entities (User, Digital Twin, Physical Twin), their deployment on separate hardware, and their specific sub-components. It correctly identifies the architectural deviation from ISO 23247 regarding the scope of the Physical Twin Entity. Supporting quotes: 'Considering that each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component' and 'The existence of this entity marks a departure from the ISO 23247 RA... can host general automotive functionality, such as Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking.'

The description accurately synthesizes the system's adherence to ISO 23247 RA guidelines, the use of ROS2 for encapsulation and middleware, the hardware separation of entities, and the specific architectural deviation concerning the Physical Twin Entity. Supporting quotes: 'implement each FE as an individual ROS2 package' and 'inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network'.

The source text describes the internal architecture of the Digital Twin System (DTS), comprising the User, Digital Twin, and Physical Twin entities communicating via ROS2 and Wi-Fi. It does not describe integration with external systems, other digital twins, or enterprise-level interoperability protocols. Therefore, the 'Not in Document' claim is correct. Supporting context: The text limits discussion to 'communication between SW components' within the ROS2 network and 'inter-entity communication' within the DTS.

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text contains specific details about models and data components. Table I explicitly describes 'Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking' (using DBSCAN and Kalman filters), 'ACC' (logic for lead vehicle and speed), and data components such as 'Data Collection', 'Data Storage' (SQL database), and 'Report Generation'.

The extraction correctly identifies the 'Vedder Electronic Speed Controller (VESC)' and the 'Actuation' software component, supported by the text: 'onboard sensors including... a Vedder Electronic Speed Controller' and 'Actuation Converts desired speed into motor voltage commands.' The description of converting speed commands into motor voltage signals is accurate. However, the extraction introduces a 'lower-level chassis controller' which is not mentioned as a specific controller managing steering and speed via VESC; the text describes the 'lower level chassis' as a hardware layer and 'Actuation' as a software component. It also adds speculative 'hardware-specific current and voltage limits' and 'closed-loop feedback from the digital twin' not explicitly detailed in the text.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text provides clear evidence of sensing infrastructure. Section III-B lists specific sensors including a 'Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR' and an 'Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera' mounted on the autonomy components.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source text mentions the general concept that 'Digital twins (DT) are real-time and high-fidelity computational reflections' in the introduction, but it does not specify fidelity requirements, validation and verification methods, uncertainty quantification, or error detection mechanisms for the specific case study system. The requirements listed (R1-R8) focus on communication, latency, and functionality rather than model fidelity or validation.

It correctly describes the Data Translation component's role in serialization and unit conversion (Section IV.C) and the use of ROS2 (Section III.B). Correctly identifies the flow from ACC Controlling FE to Actuation FE (Table I, Fig 2) and the role of the Data Translation component (Section IV.C). It accurately notes the use of ROS2 for middleware. Like the previous characteristic, it asserts 'UDP' for the transport layer, but the provided source text ends with 'using U', so this specific detail is not supported by the snippet.

Accurately extracts the use of ROS2 middleware for software components (Section III.B) and Wi-Fi for inter-entity communication (Section IV.C). II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task

block_5_judge.reasoning: The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source text mentions general lifecycle phases ('design phase', 'operation phase') only in the background section (II.B) and describes the specific development process in Section IV. It does not describe the specific Digital Twin system's lifecycle stages, representation types, transition management, or governance strategies as defined by the characteristic. Therefore, 'Not in Document' is a correct assessment.

The description correctly captures the engineering process steps mentioned in the text. It accurately references the 'requirements definition' found in Section IV-A ('we first define system requirements for the DTS') and the 'architectural design' in Section IV-B ('We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design phase'). It correctly lists the services (Data Monitoring, Computation Offloading, Remote Management) described in Section III-A. However, the claim about 'Version control' being managed through 'structured development phases with iterative testing' is speculative. The text mentions 'structured development phases' in the abstract and 'testing' in the context of requirements, but does not explicitly discuss version control or iterative testing strategies.

The description accurately reflects the decision support and user interaction capabilities detailed in the text. It matches the remote management commands ('enable/disable ACC', 'set desired speed', 'emergency braking') listed in Requirement R5 ('The UI implements the following commands: enable and disable ACC; modify the desired speed...; perform emergency braking'). It also correctly describes the real-time processing ('ACC system determines the lead vehicle, then computes a desired speed'). However, the phrase 'confidence levels derived from model validation against vehicle dynamics' is not supported by the text, as no mention of confidence levels or specific model validation for confidence is made.

The extraction provides a highly specific and detailed description of the ISO 23247 standard that perfectly matches Section II.A of the source text. It correctly lists the four parts of the standard, the entity-based architecture, and the non-prescriptive nature of implementation. Quote: 'The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview and general principles, 2) Reference architecture...' and 'The standard does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.'

block_6_judge.reasoning: The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The provided source text discusses data collection, reporting, and storage requirements (e.g., 'The DTS shall store the ego vehicle state data on a device external to the vehicle'), but does not address data governance policies, privacy protection measures, consent management, or data ownership. Therefore, the absence is correctly identified.

The extraction claims the system applies 'ISO 26262-1:2018 functional safety standards', including 'risk assessment, safety goals, and validation processes'. The source text mentions 'ISO 23247' for architecture and 'ISO 15622' for ACC performance requirements ('The DTS shall implement ACC, as outlined in ISO 15622'). It mentions 'safety-critical' domains and 'safety-critical vehicular applications', but does not mention ISO 26262, risk assessment, or safety goals. The extraction includes unsupported claims (ISO 26262) and hallucinates specific processes not found in the text.
